content.text: PII Scrubbing in Log Ingestion Pipelines

Claude integrations present a specific PII risk: user prompts may contain names, emails, addresses, medical information, or other sensitive data. If those prompts are ever accidentally logged — through debug statements, error serialisation, or framework middleware — the log store becomes a PII database with long retention and broad access. Scrubbing at the ingestion pipeline is the last line of defence after the primary control (never log content at the application layer).
This document covers the threat model, the four-stage scrubbing pipeline, configuration examples for Vector and Fluentbit, and monitoring scrub effectiveness.

attrs.alt: PII scrubbing pipeline: application → processor → PII gate → log store

content.text: Why Pipeline Scrubbing Is Necessary
Even with structured logging that explicitly avoids content fields, PII can reach logs through these vectors:
Vector
Example
Prevention
Unhandled exception serialisation
A ValidationError includes the original request body in its message
Add exception formatters that strip content fields
Third-party framework middleware
FastAPI's request logging middleware logs full request bodies
Disable or configure middleware before adding Claude routes
Debug log statements
Developer adds `logger.debug(f'prompt={prompt}')` during debugging
Scrub regex catches if it reaches logs; lint rule prevents it
SDK verbose logging
Anthropic SDK debug mode logs full request payloads
Never set SDK log level to DEBUG in production
Error response bodies
Rate limit errors include the request in their body
Scrub `request` field from error serialisations
Pipeline scrubbing is a defence-in-depth measure, not a substitute for correct application-layer logging discipline. Both are required.
The Four Scrubbing Stages
Stage 1: Drop Known Content Fields
Before any regex processing, drop JSON fields that should never appear in logs. This is the fastest and most reliable control:

# Vector configuration — drop_fields transform
[transforms.drop_content_fields]
type = "remap"
inputs = ["app_logs"]
source = '''
  # Drop fields that must never be logged
  del(.prompt)
  del(.response)
  del(.messages)
  del(.content)
  del(.input)
  del(.output)
  del(.user_message)
  del(.assistant_message)
  del(.system_prompt)
  del(.context)
  del(.documents)
  del(.retrieved_chunks)

  # If a 'request' field exists and is an object, drop its body
  if is_object(.request) {
    del(.request.body)
    del(.request.content)
  }
'''
Stage 2: Regex PII Pattern Scrubbing
After field removal, scan all remaining string values for PII patterns. Replace matches with a placeholder rather than deleting the field:

# Vector configuration — redact transform (built-in)
[transforms.redact_pii]
type = "redact"
inputs = ["drop_content_fields"]

# Built-in redactors
[[transforms.redact_pii.redactors]]
type = "us_social_security_number"

[[transforms.redact_pii.redactors]]
type = "pattern"
pattern = '[A-Za-z0-9._%+\-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.\-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}'  # email
replacement = "[EMAIL]"

[[transforms.redact_pii.redactors]]
type = "pattern"
pattern = '\b(?:\+?1[-. ]?)?(?:\(?[2-9]\d{2}\)?[-. ]?)[2-9]\d{2}[-. ]?\d{4}\b'  # US phone
replacement = "[PHONE]"

[[transforms.redact_pii.redactors]]
type = "pattern"
pattern = '\b[0-9]{13,19}\b'  # credit card (rough)
replacement = "[CARD]"

[[transforms.redact_pii.redactors]]
type = "pattern"
pattern = 'Bearer [A-Za-z0-9\-._~+/]+=*'  # auth tokens
replacement = "Bearer [REDACTED]"

[[transforms.redact_pii.redactors]]
type = "pattern"
pattern = '(?i)password["\'\s:=]+[^\s,}\'"]{6,}'  # passwords
replacement = "password=[REDACTED]"
Stage 3: Hash User Identifiers
User IDs and session IDs should be consistent (for correlation) but not reversible (for privacy). Hash them in the pipeline:

[transforms.hash_user_ids]
type = "remap"
inputs = ["redact_pii"]
source = '''
  # Hash user_id if present — preserves correlation while preventing reversal
  if exists(.user_id) && is_string(.user_id) {
    .user_id_hash = sha2(.user_id, variant: "SHA-256")[0..15]
    del(.user_id)
  }

  # Same for session_id and conversation_id
  if exists(.session_id) && is_string(.session_id) {
    .session_id_hash = sha2(.session_id, variant: "SHA-256")[0..15]
    del(.session_id)
  }
'''
Stage 4: Residual PII Gate

After scrubbing, run a second scan specifically looking for high-confidence PII patterns that should never appear in operational logs. Reject events that still contain matches and emit a metric for monitoring:

[transforms.pii_gate]
type = "remap"
inputs = ["hash_user_ids"]
source = '''
  # Serialise the full event to check for residual PII in any field
  encoded = encode_json(.)

  # SSN pattern — very high confidence, should never appear
  if match(encoded, r'\b\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}\b') {
    .scrub_rejected = true
    .scrub_reason = "ssn_pattern"
  }

  # Raw email in any remaining string field
  if match(encoded, r'[A-Za-z0-9._%+\-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.\-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}') {
    .scrub_rejected = true
    .scrub_reason = coalesce(.scrub_reason, "email_pattern")
  }
'''

[transforms.split_gate]
type = "route"
inputs = ["pii_gate"]

[transforms.split_gate.route]
clean = ".scrub_rejected == null || .scrub_rejected == false"
rejected = ".scrub_rejected == true"
Handling Pipeline Rejections
Rejected events must not be silently dropped — they indicate a logging bug that needs fixing. Route them to a separate, restricted stream and emit a Prometheus metric:

# Route clean events to Loki
[sinks.loki_clean]
type = "loki"
inputs = ["split_gate.clean"]
endpoint = "http://loki:3100"

[sinks.loki_clean.labels]
service = "{{ service }}"
env = "{{ env }}"

# Route rejections to a restricted audit sink + a metrics counter
[sinks.audit_rejected]
type = "http"
inputs = ["split_gate.rejected"]
uri = "https://internal-audit-store/ingest"
# This endpoint requires a separate token with audit-write scope
auth.strategy = "bearer"
auth.token = "$AUDIT_STORE_TOKEN"

[transforms.scrub_metrics]
type = "log_to_metric"
inputs = ["split_gate.rejected"]

[[transforms.scrub_metrics.metrics]]
type = "counter"
field = "scrub_reason"
name = "log_pii_scrub_rejection_total"

[transforms.scrub_metrics.metrics.tags]
service = "{{ service }}"
reason = "{{ scrub_reason }}"

[sinks.prometheus_remote_write]
type = "prometheus_remote_write"
inputs = ["scrub_metrics"]
endpoint = "http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/write"
The audit sink for rejected events requires
separate
access controls from the main log store. Fewer engineers should have read access to it — the rejected events potentially contain the PII that triggered the rejection.
Fluentbit Configuration Equivalent
For teams using Fluentbit instead of Vector, the equivalent Lua-based scrubbing:

# fluent-bit.conf

[SERVICE]
    Flush 5
    Log_Level warn

[INPUT]
    Name   forward
    Listen 0.0.0.0
    Port   24224

# Stage 1: Drop content fields using Lua
[FILTER]
    Name   lua
    Match  claude.*
    script /etc/fluent-bit/scripts/drop_content.lua
    call   drop_content_fields

# Stage 2: Regex scrubbing using modify + sed-style replacements
[FILTER]
    Name  modify
    Match claude.*
    # Replace email addresses
    Set_Regex email .*@.*\..{2,4} [EMAIL]

# Stage 3: Hash user_id using Lua
[FILTER]
    Name   lua
    Match  claude.*
    script /etc/fluent-bit/scripts/hash_ids.lua
    call   hash_user_id

[OUTPUT]
    Name  loki
    Match claude.*
    host  loki
    port  3100

-- /etc/fluent-bit/scripts/drop_content.lua
function drop_content_fields(tag, timestamp, record)
    local fields_to_drop = {
        "prompt", "response", "messages", "content",
        "input", "output", "user_message", "assistant_message",
        "system_prompt", "context", "documents"
    }
    for _, field in ipairs(fields_to_drop) do
        record[field] = nil
    end
    return 1, timestamp, record
end
Monitoring Scrubbing Effectiveness
The scrubbing pipeline itself needs to be observed. Track these metrics:
Metric
Type
Alert On
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'log_pii_scrub_rejection_total', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}
Counter by service + reason
Any rejections — investigate within 24h
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'log_pipeline_events_processed_total', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}
Counter by stage
Drop > 5% (field removal is working correctly)
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'log_pipeline_stage_latency_seconds', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}
Histogram
p99 > 50ms (scrubbing is slowing ingestion)
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'log_events_per_second', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}
Gauge
Drop > 20% (application stopped logging)

# Prometheus alert for PII reaching the log pipeline
groups:
  - name: pii_scrubbing
    rules:
      - alert: PIIScrubRejections
        expr: increase(log_pii_scrub_rejection_total[1h]) > 0
        for: 0m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "PII pattern found in Claude service logs for {{ $labels.service }}"
          description: >
            {{ $value }} log events contained PII patterns and were rejected by the
            scrubbing pipeline in the last hour. Reason: {{ $labels.reason }}.
            Investigate the logging code for the affected service.
          runbook: https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/pii-in-logs
Testing the Scrubbing Pipeline
Test scrubbing rules before deploying them to production. Create synthetic log events that contain PII and verify they are correctly redacted:

# tests/test_scrubbing.py
import json, subprocess, pytest

VECTOR_CONFIG = "vector.toml"


def run_through_pipeline(event: dict) -> dict | None:
    """Feed a single event through the Vector pipeline and return the output."""
    input_line = json.dumps(event)
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["vector", "--config", VECTOR_CONFIG, "--quiet"],
        input=input_line,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
        timeout=5,
    )
    lines = [l for l in result.stdout.strip().split("\n") if l]
    return json.loads(lines[-1]) if lines else None


@pytest.mark.parametrize("field", ["prompt", "response", "messages", "content"])
def test_content_field_dropped(field):
    event = {"event": "api_call_complete", "service": "claude-proxy", field: "some content"}
    out = run_through_pipeline(event)
    assert out is not None
    assert field not in out, f"Field '{field}' was not dropped by the pipeline"


def test_email_redacted():
    event = {"event": "request_received", "service": "claude-proxy",
             "description": "User john.doe@example.com submitted a request"}
    out = run_through_pipeline(event)
    assert "[EMAIL]" in out.get("description", "")
    assert "@example.com" not in json.dumps(out)


def test_user_id_hashed():
    event = {"event": "request_received", "service": "claude-proxy",
             "user_id": "user-12345"}
    out = run_through_pipeline(event)
    assert "user_id" not in out
    assert "user_id_hash" in out
    assert out["user_id_hash"] != "user-12345"


def test_clean_event_passes_gate():
    event = {"event": "api_call_complete", "service": "claude-proxy",
             "output_tokens": 150, "duration_ms": 1234}
    out = run_through_pipeline(event)
    assert out is not None
    assert out.get("scrub_rejected") is not True
Policy and Compliance Checklist
Use this checklist when deploying or auditing the PII scrubbing pipeline:
All four scrubbing stages are deployed and validated with synthetic PII test events.
Pipeline rejection metrics are alerting to the security team, not just the on-call engineer.
The audit sink for rejected events has separate access controls from the main log store.
Scrubbing configuration is version-controlled and deployed via CI — not manually edited.
The log retention policy is documented: INFO+ logs for 30 days, DEBUG logs for 3 days.
A data subject access request (DSAR) playbook exists that lists which log streams to include.
The scrubbing test suite runs in CI before every pipeline config change.
Third-party SDK debug logging is explicitly disabled in production environment config.

Even with a perfect scrubbing pipeline, never rely on it as the primary PII control. The structured logging schema (previous document) defines which fields exist — scrubbing is a backstop for when those constraints are violated.
Summary

PII scrubbing in the log ingestion pipeline provides defence-in-depth against accidental content logging in Claude integrations. The four-stage pipeline — drop known content fields, regex-scrub PII patterns, hash user identifiers, validate with a residual PII gate — applies equally to Vector and Fluentbit. Rejected events are never silently discarded: they are routed to a restricted audit stream and increment a Prometheus counter that alerts the security team.
Stage 1 (field drops) is the highest-signal control — it removes entire categories of risk.
Stage 2 (regex) catches accidental content inclusion in other fields; keep patterns simple to avoid false positives.
Stage 3 (ID hashing) ensures user correlation is possible without storing reversible identifiers.
Stage 4 (gate) is the final check; any rejection indicates an application-layer logging bug that needs a code fix.
Test the pipeline with synthetic PII events in CI before every configuration change.
